explanations.1: As stated in the prelude, particle physics deals with the substructures of atoms and nuclei to find the most fundamental building blocks.
explanations.2: Yukawa’s 1935 proposal suggested that a particle now known as a pion is exchanged between nucleons to carry the strong force.
explanations.3: Using $\Delta E \Delta t \approx h/4\pi$ and substituting $\Delta E = mc^2$, the time a virtual particle exists is inversely proportional to its mass-energy.
explanations.4: Force range is limited by the time a virtual particle can exist; massless particles like photons can exist indefinitely, resulting in an infinite range.
explanations.5: Muons have a mass of $106\text{ MeV}/c^2$ (near the predicted $100\text{ MeV}/c^2$), but they only interact via the weak force and EM force, not the strong force.
explanations.6: Gravity has a relative strength of $10^{-38}$ compared to the strong force, making it the weakest of the four.
explanations.7: The weak nuclear force is carried by the massive $W$ and $Z$ bosons, which accounts for its extremely short range.
explanations.8: Feynman diagrams represent time and position to describe how particles exchange virtual carrier particles during interactions.
explanations.9: In a fixed-target collision, much energy is lost to recoil kinetic energy, whereas head-on collisions allow nearly all energy to go into particle creation.
explanations.10: Synchrotrons keep the orbital radius constant by increasing magnetic field strength in sync with the particle's increasing energy.
explanations.11: Total energy (250 MeV) divided by the number of gaps (500) equals $0.5\text{ MeV}$ per gap, which requires $500,000\text{ Volts}$.
explanations.12: The defining characteristic of hadrons is that they interact via the strong nuclear force, whereas leptons do not.
explanations.13: Matter-antimatter pairs annihilate upon contact, typically producing photons representing the total mass-energy of the pair.
explanations.14: Protons have a baryon number of $+1$ and must decay into another baryon to conserve the total baryon number.
explanations.15: Mesons are composed of one quark and one antiquark, resulting in a baryon number of zero and integral spin.
explanations.16: Strangeness is conserved in strong and electromagnetic interactions but can be violated in weak interactions.
explanations.17: Up, charmed, and top quarks carry a charge of $+2/3$ the elementary charge, while down, strange, and bottom quarks carry $-1/3$.
explanations.18: A neutron is composed of one up quark ($+2/3$) and two down quarks ($-1/3$ each), resulting in a total charge of zero.
explanations.19: Color is a property similar to charge that comes in three types (red, green, blue) and explains why quarks obey the Pauli exclusion principle.
explanations.20: The scattering of 20-GeV electrons from protons at SLAC showed three point-like charges inside the proton, analogous to Rutherford’s discovery of the nucleus.
explanations.21: Only the weak nuclear force can change the flavor of a quark, which is why it is responsible for beta decay.
explanations.22: Electroweak theory proposed that the electromagnetic and weak forces are identical at sufficiently high energies.
explanations.23: Quantum Chromodynamics calls for eight massless gluons, six of which carry color/anticolor combinations and two that do not.
explanations.24: GUTs describe the unification of the strong force with the already unified electroweak force at very high energy scales.
explanations.25: The existence of the massive Higgs boson explains the origin of mass for $W$ and $Z$ bosons and supports electroweak symmetry breaking.
explanations.26: Under normal circumstances, the strong force is the most powerful ($1$), followed by EM ($10^{-2}$), weak ($10^{-13}$), and gravity ($10^{-38}$).
explanations.27: An antibaryon is made of three antiquarks, each with $B = -1/3$, leading to a total baryon number of $-1$.
explanations.28: The TOE point, where gravity unifies with the GUT force, is estimated to occur at the Planck scale of approximately $10^{19}\text{ GeV}$.
